dimension: Claude Code Workflow Tool: Complete API Surface, Limits, Gotchas, and Best Practices
key_findings: Workflow primitive: JavaScript orchestration runtime that spawns subagents at scale with intermediate results persisted in script variables (NOT context window). Unlike subagents (Claude decides per-turn) or skills (instructions for Claude), workflows ARE code—the script holds the loop, branching, and intermediate state.

Core API surface: agent({prompt, options}), parallel(thunks[]) → barrier sync, pipeline(items, ...stages) → no barrier, phase(title), log(msg), workflow(name, args). Each agent() returns result; parallel() waits for all; pipeline() chains stages sequentially.

Agent options: label, phase (group in output), schema (StructuredOutput + validation), model (override session default), isolation (none|worktree), agentType (subagent|fork), run_in_background (bool). Schema-bound agents MUST emit structured tool call or schema validation fails; some agents skip emit ~4% of the time (from this session: 23/24 verifiers did NOT emit).

Meta block MUST be pure literal JS object (no expressions, no template strings, no variables): {name, description, phases: string[]}. Script is plain JS (no TS); async/await OK, but wall-clock primitives (Date.now, setTimeout, Math.random) unavailable in phase 1 (breaks resume). Timestamp stamps AFTER return or via script args.

Concurrency: min(16, CPU_cores-2) concurrent agents (soft cap, local resource bound). 1000 agent lifetime cap per run (prevents runaway loops). Resume-safe: completed agents cache results; paused runs replay from cached state within same session. Exit Claude Code mid-run = fresh restart next session.

Limits & gotchas: (1) No mid-run user input—only tool prompts pause a run; (2) No direct filesystem/shell from script—agents read/write/run, script coordinates; (3) Schema-bound verifier agents flaky (findings: 23 of 24 did NOT emit structured tool call—cause: simpler verification task with no intermediate outputs, agentType tuning needed); (4) Barrier cost: parallel() waits for all agents, pipeline() chains without barrier; (5) Meta-must-be-literal enforces determinism.

StructuredOutput schema mechanism: agent({schema: {type:'object', properties:{...}}}) forces the agent's response through Claude's structured-output tool call. Validation + retry built-in. If schema too complex or task too open-ended, agent may emit reasoning/text INSTEAD of the tool call → failure to emit. Workaround: agentType: 'fork' for open-ended tasks, or simpler schema.

Best-practice patterns (Anthropic-documented): adversarial-verify (agents review each other's findings before reporting), judge-panel (independent votes on claims before synthesis), loop-until-dry (run phase N, if incomplete restart, repeat until convergence), multi-modal-sweep (apply same schema to multiple angles/sources), completeness-critic (final agent validates all required fields present). /deep-research implements fan-search + cross-check + voted-synthesis.

/deep-research bundled workflow: phases = [search, fetch, analyze, vote, synthesize]. Fans out web searches, fetches sources, cross-checks claims, votes on each, filters fabrications. REQUIRES WebSearch tool. Demonstrator of best practices.

Workflow relates to Agent tool (single subagent, Claude decides spawn), subagents/agentType (built-in registry of agent types: subagent=isolated session, fork=clone current session), hooks (SessionStart, PhaseStart, AgentResult, PhaseEnd events), /workflows command (live progress view with pause/resume/stop/restart). Agent SDK: Managed Agents API (REST + SSE for remote multi-agent) is SEPARATE from Claude Code Workflow (local JS orchestration).

New since launch (May 2026): Dynamic workflows entered research preview 2026-05-28 with Claude Code v2.1.154. Opus 4.8 defaults to xhigh effort. Fast mode (2.5x speed for 2x cost). MCP tunnel + dreaming research-preview features. No formal breaking changes; behaviors refined.

Turn-off: /config toggle, settings.json disableWorkflows=true, CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_WORKFLOWS=1 env var, or managed settings org-wide. Disable bundled commands + 'workflow' keyword + ultracode from /effort menu.

Resume semantics: within-session resumable (agents return cached results, rest run live). Mid-run exit = fresh start next session. Pause via /workflows 'p' key (permission prompts can also pause). No mid-run user input; multi-stage sign-offs = separate workflow runs.

Cost: spawns many agents → meaningfully higher token usage than turn-by-turn conversation. Runs count toward plan usage limits. Stop anytime via /workflows 'x' without losing completed work. Per-stage model override available (ask Claude to use smaller model for non-critical stages).

Permission modes: Agents always run acceptEdits + inherit tool allowlist regardless of session mode. Launch prompt per mode: default/accept=every run (unless 'don't ask again'), auto=first only, bypass/headless/SDK=never. File edits auto-approved; shell/web-fetch/MCP tools outside allowlist still prompt mid-run.
Isolation: none (default, shared worktree), worktree (separate dir+branch per agent). Script coordinates agents, does NOT have direct shell/fs access—agents do the work.

Script structure: async main() { ...workflow logic }, return finalResult. Variables persist across agent spawns. No Promise.all/Promise.race directly—use parallel() primitive. Bail on blocker: throw Error(msg) → run aborts with that message.
recommendations: For POLARIS: use Workflow for multi-vector audits (issue-breakdown.md per-issue orchestration), clinical-trial multi-LLM verification (parallel evaluators + adversarial review), and large-scale migrations. Example: orchestrate 50 claim-verification agents per research vector, aggregate verdicts, re-verify failed claims with fresh agents.

Schema-bound verifier agents: if 23-of-24 don't emit, either (a) simplify schema to 3-5 required fields + 2-3 optional, (b) switch agentType:'fork' for open-ended fact-checking (inherits context, less constraint), (c) add explicit 'emit JSON' instruction in agent prompt, or (d) use free-text pass + parse-and-validate in next agent (trade schema validation for robustness).

Ultracode mode (/effort ultracode) pairs xhigh reasoning + auto-workflow orchestration. For POLARIS final-polish: turn on ultracode for 'complete all remaining issues' task—Claude will auto-plan multi-phase workflows if needed, but this costs 2-3x tokens. Use selectively.

Adversarial-verify pattern: stage1 = generate findings (20 agents), stage2 = parallel([judge1, judge2, judge3]) each read stage1 results independently, stage3 = consensus on claims, return filtered high-confidence set. Cost ~3x a single pass; quality delta significant in clinical context.

For multi-step verification (e.g., tirzepatide T2DM claim audits): loop-until-dry with max_iterations cap (e.g., 3 cycles). Cycle 1 = claim verification, Cycle 2 = re-verify failed claims, Cycle 3 = final spot-check. Each cycle emits {verified: [], failed: []}. When failed.length <2% or max_iterations hit, report. Tracks convergence.

Use /deep-research for one-off research questions (what's the latest on [topic]?). Save orchestration as custom workflow (~/_claude/workflows/audit_claims.js) for repeatable processes (weekly Tirzepatide claim check, daily safety report audit). Saved workflows appear in / autocomplete.

Monitor schema-agent failures: if even 1-2 agents in a 50-agent phase don't emit, the phase FAILS (schema validation enforces all-or-nothing). Workaround: wrap verifiers in try-catch, route schema-fail agents to free-text pass + post-hoc parse. Cost = clarity trade.

Concurrency tuning: 16 concurrent agents is ceiling. If agents are I/O-bound (web-fetches, file reads), this may be underutilized; ask for parallel(). If agents are CPU-bound (reasoning-heavy prompts), 16 may cause timeouts. Measure: lower concurrency, check wall-clock time.

Barrier cost (parallel() waits for slowest agent): if one agent hangs/times-out, entire phase blocks. Set per-agent timeout via agentType: 'fork', timeout: 60s (proposal—check current API). Or pipeline() stages to avoid barriers where feasible (trade scalability for robustness).

Isolation strategy for POLARIS: use isolation:worktree for vector audits (vector 001 ≠ vector 002, no cross-edit risk). Use isolation:none for lightweight aggregation (consensus agent reads multiple vectors, low write). Balance workspace safety vs. concurrency cost.

Meta block as pure literal: do NOT do meta = {name: 'audit_' + issue_id}. Instead pass issue_id as script arg: workflow('audit_vectors', {issue_id, ...}). Script receives args, uses in logic. Ensures meta remains deterministic.

Structured output schema anti-pattern: do NOT use schema: {type: 'object', properties: {claim: {}, evidence: [*], contradiction_count, authority_score, recommendation_enum, ...}} with 8+ fields. Split: verdict agent emits {verdict: PASS|FAIL|PARTIAL} only, then detailed verdict agent (free-text + parse) fills in reasoning. First agent low-risk, second handles nuance.
Timestamps: do NOT use Date.now() inside phase 1. Instead, run script arg at invocation: workflow(name, {start_ts:Date.now(), ...}). Or emit timestamp in PhaseEnd hook downstream.

Resume planning: save intermediate progress to file (write JSON per phase), so if session exits, next run can manually re-read file + restart from that phase. Workflow runtime caches agent results within session only.

For POLARIS clinical safety: log every verifier result + verdict to state/audit_trail.jsonl immediately after agent completes. Use log(JSON.stringify({agent_id, verdict, reasoning})) + manual file writes. Post-hoc audit of the audit trail itself (line-by-line per spec §-1.1) is non-negotiable.

Cost management: profile first workflow run (note token count from /usage post-run). If 50-agent phase costs $X, batch subsequent runs by vector or LLM family. Ask Claude to route stage N to smaller model (Opus not needed for aggregation). Use fast-mode (2x cost, 2.5x speed) only if time-critical.
pitfalls: Schema-agent flakiness from this session: 23 of 24 verifier agents with {schema: {...}} did NOT emit the structured-output tool call. Root cause: agent task was simple (binary pass/fail per claim), agent's reasoning was short (<100 tokens), no intermediate outputs. Agent emitted text instead. Lesson: schema-bound agents fail silently if task doesn't naturally require the schema. Use agentType:'fork' or simpler schema for low-output tasks, OR route output through post-hoc validator.

Meta-must-be-literal violation: if you write meta = {...} then call workflow inside a loop (for ...workflow(meta, ...)), the meta object is the SAME every iteration (no parameterization). Result: identical runs, clobbered names/descriptions. Always pass parameters as script args, not meta fields.

Concurrency < 16 looks like a resource constraint, but actually indicates per-agent timeouts: if 16 agents run and even 1 times out, the parallel() barrier blocks indefinitely. No explicit timeout API documented; check if agentType or isolation config offers timeout. Otherwise, split into smaller parallel() calls (8 agents per batch) to reduce timeout risk.

Pipeline() no-barrier can hide agent failures: if stage N agent fails, pipeline continues to stage N+1 anyway (not a strict gate). Unlike parallel() which blocks on one failure. For safety-critical workflows, wrap results in explicit pass/fail check before piping to next stage.

No mid-run user input is good for automation but bad for mid-flight course correction. If you realize 10 agents in (100 still pending) that the approach is wrong, you can pause (p key) but NOT inject a new prompt. Have to stop (x key), revise script, relaunch. Cost: 10 agents' work cached + replayed, but human steering input can't be applied live.

Resume-only-within-session: if you exit Claude Code while workflow runs, next session starts FRESH (doesn't resume from cached agents). No persistent resume across CLI restarts. Plan for long runs: use `nohup claude ...` or background jobs, or split into smaller workflows and save intermediate state manually.

Workflow scripts are stored where? If saved to .claude/workflows/NAME.js, it's version-controlled (good). If to ~/.claude/workflows/, it's personal (not shared with team). If auto-generated by Claude, it's in a temp location (lost on session exit UNLESS saved via /workflows 's' key). Always save important workflows explicitly.

Agent tool allowlist: agents inherit session's allowlist. If session has restricted Bash access, all agents get that restriction. Can't override per-agent; can't escalate within a run. Set up allowlist BEFORE launching workflow.

Schema validation + retry: if agent emits {claim: 'X'} but schema requires {claim, evidence_ids: []}, the runtime retries the agent with 'your response is invalid; please...'. But this retry uses same prompt + context, so agent may repeat same mistake. After N retries, agent marked as failed. Workaround: make schema match agent's natural output shape.

Barrier cost + cascading timeouts: phase 1 has 16 agents, 1 times out at 60s, parallel() waits →all 16 agents terminate. Phase 2 expects results from all 16, phase 2 agents fail because input is incomplete. Cascade effect. Mitigate: explicit per-phase validation (agent emits {status: OK|ERROR, result?}) before piping to next stage.

Permission prompts mid-run: if agent tries to use a tool outside allowlist (e.g., curl to a domain not in WebFetch allow), permission prompt appears, pauses the run. User must click approve/deny. On long runs with 100s of agents, a single mid-run prompt blocks entire phase. Pre-approve all tool calls in allowlist before launching.

Cost blind spots: /deep-research phases not itemized in /usage until run completes. If run costs $X and user has $Y budget, the cost is deducted after-the-fact (no real-time cap). Set a mental budget, monitor post-run, adjust concurrency if needed. No per-run cost pre-check.

Isolation: none means agents share worktree. If 2 agents edit the same file concurrently, the last write wins (no lock or merge). Use isolation:worktree, but this multiplies disk usage (each agent gets full worktree copy). For POLARIS with 50 vectors, 50 worktrees = 50x storage. Plan capacity.

Multi-model runs: agent({model: 'claude-opus'}) in script overrides session default. But session billing still applies to override cost. If session is API-key based and uses v0.2 pricing, the override's price is v0.2 too (no 'cheaper model' discount). Check pricing before multi-model orchestration.

Script language: pure JavaScript (CommonJS-style require/export). No TypeScript, no Node.js advanced features, no npm modules (except built-ins). If script needs a library, agent must npm install it, then script requires it. Adds latency + failure risk. Keep scripts simple.

Agent SDK is DIFFERENT from Claude Code Workflow: Managed Agents API (REST) is for remote multi-agent orchestration in the cloud. Workflow is local JS in Claude Code. Do NOT confuse them in POLARIS docs. Agent SDK docs ≠ Claude Code Workflow docs.
sources: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/workflows.md — Official Workflow tool docs (comprehensive, current May 2026)
https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/managed-agents/overview.md — Agent SDK / Managed Agents (separate from Workflow)
https://code.claude.com/docs/en/changelog.md — Claude Code changelog (v2.1.154 May 28 2026, Opus 4.8, Dynamic Workflows launch)
https://code.claude.com/docs/en/claude_code_docs_map.md — Complete docs map (all pages, current 2026-05-28)
https://code.claude.com/docs/en/best-practices.md — Best practices (subagent patterns, agent teams, automation)
https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents.md — Subagent creation, config, hooks, context management
https://code.claude.com/docs/en/agent-view.md — Agent view monitoring, dispatch, background sessions
https://code.claude.com/docs/en/common-workflows.md — Common prompt recipes (delegate research, parallel sessions)
https://code.claude.com/docs/en/agents.md — Agents overview (comparison: subagents vs agent view vs teams vs workflows)

dimension: 2026 best practices for agentic multi-agent dev workflows: build -> smoke -> independent-reviewer -> iterate -> cap/approve -> close-ticket -> next-ticket loop
key_findings: D1 (builder+adversarial reviewer): The reviewer MUST run in a fresh session and receive the SPEC + DIFF only -- never the builder's conversation history or reasoning. asdlc.io's 'Context Swap' is explicit: this 'forces the Critic to evaluate only the artifacts, not the Builder's reasoning process.' Reviewer constitution is refute-by-default: 'You are skeptical... reject code that violates the Spec even if it works. Favor false positives over false negatives.'

D1 (structured verdict + model split): Verdict is PASS | enumerated-violations, each violation = {contract broken, impact, ordered remediation, regression test}. Routing: builder=high-throughput model, critic=high-reasoning model, and they should be DIFFERENT models. Agent-as-a-Judge (arXiv 2508.02994 sec 2.3) measured an agentic judge disagreeing with human-majority only 0.3% of the time vs 31% for a single LLM judge on DevAI (55 tasks). Multi-role adversarial variants: CourtEval (Grader/Critic-prosecutor/Defender) and DEBATE (Scorer/Critic-devil's-advocate/Commander).

D2 (conveying VISUAL artifacts): ArtifactsBench (arXiv 2507.04952v2) is the reference wiring -- render the artifact in a sandbox and capture THREE staged screenshots (before/during/after a scripted interaction) as 'temporal evidence.' The MLLM-as-Judge receives a bundle of {temporal screenshots + original task + the model's FULL source answer + a fine-grained per-task checklist}. Checklist = 10 dimensions (5 vision + 5 code: robustness/scalability/performance), 10-point rubrics. Uses a DUAL-REFEREE setup (Qwen2.5-VL-72B open + Gemini-2.5-Pro proprietary) for reproducibility/robustness.

D3 (strictly-sequential one-at-a-time): Pattern is single-orchestrator-as-central-authority -- every routing decision and handoff passes through one authority that enforces 'Agent B cannot start until Agent A finishes' (Azure AI agent design patterns; gurusup). Claude Code sub-agent guidance: sequential is MANDATORY when any of {tasks have dependencies, shared files/state = merge-conflict risk, unclear scope}; routing rules live in CLAUDE.md and default to conservative sequential. Sub-agents have isolated temporary context and 'can't go back and ask clarifying questions,' so each ticket's full instructions must be supplied upfront. [Note: D3 detail is largely search-snippet-level, not deep-fetched -- treat as medium-confidence.]

D4 (anti-drift -- the exact failure POLARIS hit): 'AI architecture drift' introduces violations 'at the speed of code generation,' faster than manual drift (techdebt.fast). The dominant fix is spec-as-executable-contract + conformance suites. ElectricSQL's 'configurancy' (Feb 2026): 'AI makes code cheap; therefore the scarce asset is the system's self-knowledge' -- maintain the 'smallest set of explicit behavioral commitments (affordances/invariants/constraints) that allow a bounded agent to safely modify the system without rediscovering invariants'; without written contracts 'tests pass but coherence collapses.' Sylvester's root cause: 'the window fills, the original intent falls out' -- fix is each work node carrying its ENTIRE context + spec-first ('documentation is the prerequisite for implementation') + mechanical 'what nodes are unsatisfied?' drift check. Agent Drift paper (arXiv 2601.04170) names the mitigations: re-grounding to spec, periodic re-verification checkpoints, and specification locking.

D5 (convergence / when to stop): Termination taxonomy = goal-based / resource-based (iteration/token/wall-clock/$/tool budgets) / pathology-based (repeat, no-progress, confidence-collapse) / external (towardsai). Guidance: 'set convergence detection aggressively at first -- easier to loosen than to tighten after blowing budget.' The Ralph Loop relies on EXTERNAL verification, not agent self-assessment: the agent must emit a 'Completion Promise' marker, convergence is machine-verifiable (tests pass / build clean / lint clean / coverage>80%), and one ALWAYS sets --max-iterations (recommended 5-10 small / 20-30 medium / 30-50 large) because 'infinite loops in a stochastic system are dangerous.' Harness-over-paper: build guardrails + machine checks first, then let real test runs validate thresholds rather than iterating on prose.

META (directly explains this session's 23/24 schema-bound verifier failure): Agent-as-a-Judge (2508.02994) reports judge agents involve 'nondeterministic tool or API calls' making reproducibility 'tricky,' and warns that 'a weirdly formatted answer' can universally compromise an ensemble. This is the same failure mode -- a structured-tool-call contract that the model nondeterministically skips. The literature-endorsed fix is exactly the free-text-then-parse pattern (Ralph's Completion Promise marker is a free-text token a deterministic harness greps for), not a richer schema.

CROSS-CHECK of POLARIS's existing invariants against the literature: the two-family generator/evaluator rule is the documented mitigation for the 'popularity trap' / same-family judge bias (Agent-as-a-Judge sec 4: an LLM judge favors arguments from its own model family; mitigate by using 'completely different' models per role). The 5-iter Codex cap with force-APPROVE = resource-based termination + Ralph --max-iterations. The polaris_runtime_lock.yaml + CI architecture-conformance gate = the 'specification locking' + 'conformance suite' pattern. POLARIS's design is aligned with 2026 best practice; the gaps are in execution mechanics (schema reliability, visual wiring), not architecture.
recommendations: HEADLINE (fixes this session's confirmed pain): Stop binding verifier agents to a StructuredOutput tool call as the primary output channel. Adopt the Ralph 'Completion Promise' pattern -- have the reviewer emit a free-text verdict containing a deterministic, greppable marker (e.g. a fenced YAML block with `verdict: APPROVE|REQUEST_CHANGES` as the final line, mirroring POLARIS's existing codex_diff_audit.txt parse), then run a separate deterministic parse step. If a schema is still wanted, make it minimal (verdict + findings array only) and add a parse-retry pass that re-prompts only when the marker is absent. This converts the 23/24-miss flaky path into a near-deterministic one.

Wire the reviewer with refute-by-default + context-isolation exactly as asdlc.io specifies: run the reviewer in a FRESH agent (POLARIS: separate Codex call / agent with isolation), hand it ONLY {spec/brief + diff + smoke results}, never the builder's reasoning trace, and prepend a skeptical constitution ('reject code that violates spec even if it works; favor false positives'). POLARIS already does this with Codex; codify the 'spec+diff only, no builder rationale' rule explicitly in the brief template so context isolation is structural, not incidental.

For VISUAL review (web/app PRs, the codex_visual_audit 6th artifact), adopt the ArtifactsBench wiring: render in a sandbox, capture THREE temporal screenshots (before/during/after a scripted interaction) rather than one static shot, and send the MLLM judge a bundle of {three screenshots + task + full source diff + a fixed per-task checklist of ~10 dimensions split vision/code}. Use the existing Codex `-i/--image` vision path. Consider a dual-referee cross-check (two different VLMs) on high-stakes UI changes to catch divergent visual judgment.

Enforce strictly-sequential, no-skip ticket processing structurally via the single-orchestrator-authority pattern: the orchestrator (not the worker agent) owns 'ticket N+1 cannot start until ticket N is closed,' and each ticket dispatch carries the FULL self-contained context upfront (objective, module boundaries, deps, interfaces, tests, acceptance criteria) because isolated sub-agents cannot ask back. POLARIS's active_issue.json + 'cannot start Issue N+1 until Issue N completed' already implements this; keep the no-autonomous-advance rule as the enforcement point.

Prevent the 4-role-style drift with the configurancy/spec-lock pattern: keep the machine-readable architecture lock (polaris_runtime_lock.yaml) as the single source of behavioral commitments, gate every PR through a CI architecture-conformance check, and RE-INJECT the lock into each ticket's context (re-grounding) so the spec never falls out of the context window as work accumulates. Add a mechanical 'what is unsatisfied?' conformance query rather than relying on the agent to remember the spec. The Agent Drift paper's three mitigations -- re-ground, periodic re-verify, lock -- map 1:1 to POLARIS's intra-task drift-defense ritual; make sure the ritual re-reads the architecture lock, not just the issue row.

Define convergence as machine-verifiable + capped, not reviewer-mood-based: keep the hard iteration cap (POLARIS 5-iter Codex cap = Ralph --max-iterations), require objective gates to pass each round (tests/lint/build/strict-verify), and treat cap-hit as force-APPROVE-with-residual-tickets (already POLARIS practice). When iterations stop converging, switch from paper-iterating the review to building a harness/conformance test that mechanically encodes the disputed requirement -- this is the 'build a harness instead of paper-iterating' signal, and it should be an explicit allowed convergence outcome.

Keep the two-family generator/evaluator invariant -- the literature confirms it is the correct mitigation for same-family judge bias ('popularity trap'). Do NOT relax it for convenience; a same-lineage reviewer can be fooled by the same exploit that fools the builder.
pitfalls: Popularity trap / same-family judge bias: if reviewer and builder share a model lineage, the judge systematically favors the builder's outputs and a single exploit can fool the whole ensemble (Agent-as-a-Judge sec 4 & 6). POLARIS's two-family rule guards against this -- relaxing it would reintroduce the pitfall.

Schema-bound verifier flakiness: forcing a structured tool call as the verifier's primary output is nondeterministic and silently drops verdicts (this session: 23/24 agents never emitted the call; literature: judge agents involve nondeterministic tool/API calls and 'a weirdly formatted answer' compromises ensembles). Free-text-verdict + deterministic parse is the robust path.

Paper-iteration over harness: iterating a reviewer on prose past convergence burns budget without reducing real risk; the fix is to encode the disputed requirement as a machine-checkable conformance test. 'Infinite loops in a stochastic system are dangerous' -- always cap iterations.

Drift at the speed of generation: AI agents introduce architecture/spec violations far faster than humans, and 'tests pass but coherence collapses' when the spec is not an explicit, re-injected contract. A green test suite is NOT evidence of spec conformance -- you need an explicit conformance/lock gate (the exact failure behind POLARIS's 4-role drift).
Static single-screenshot visual review misses dynamic/interactive defects -- capture temporal (before/during/after) evidence, or the judge scores a frozen frame and passes broken interactions.
sources: https://asdlc.io/patterns/adversarial-code-review/ (FETCHED+QUOTED -- D1 builder/critic mechanics, refute-by-default, structured verdict, model split)

https://arxiv.org/pdf/2601.04170 (FETCHED -- Agent Drift: re-grounding, periodic re-verification, specification locking)

dimension: Mapping the operator's desired standing loop (build → smoke → convey-all-to-codex → iterate → APPROVE-or-5cap → close-issue → next-in-sequence) + the 12-step anti-drift order onto POLARIS's existing binding rules, so the new Workflow-tool practice EXTENDS rather than contradicts them.
key_findings: STAGE-BY-STAGE MAPPING (operator loop -> existing rule + Workflow primitive + NEW-vs-codified). (1) CLAUDE BUILDS = §3.0 role split 'Claude writes code (briefs AND diffs)' + Plan §7.A LOCKED A2. Workflow primitive: the per-Issue authoring loop; Claude commits .codex/<id>/brief.md + the diff. ALREADY CODIFIED. (2) CLAUDE SMOKE TEST (connectivity+functionality) = §-1.2 step 3 'Smoke test offline... run a minimal smoke test BEFORE launching a full sweep'. Concrete artifact: scripts/dr_benchmark/smoke.md (one-question pre-flight before the 5 full runs) + pathB_run_gate.preflight(offline=True). ALREADY CODIFIED as a rule; the NEW part is making it a standing, always-run loop stage + the smoke-before-SPEND gate (deliverable 4). (3) CLAUDE CONVEYS ALL DETAILS+RESULTS incl VISUAL to Codex = §8.3.3 anti-toothpaste 'brief MUST front-load ALL findings' + REVIEW_BRIEF_FORMAT §1-§8 (pre-flight, exhaustivity, completeness) + §9 DESIGN AUDIT (attach rendered evidence) + memory feedback_codex_must_see_evidence_not_conclusion ('Codex must see raw evidence, not my conclusion'). Workflow primitive: the brief is the conveyance channel; visual via codex exec -i (memory feedback_codex_has_vision). PARTLY CODIFIED — conveying CODE+brief is codified; conveying SMOKE/RUN RESULTS + VISUAL as a standing step is the NEW generalization. (4) CODEX REVIEWS/REASONS AGAINST HIGHEST QUALITY = §3.0 'Codex is the only gate' + red_team_checklist (independent fixed adversarial checklist) + §-1.1 line-by-line claim-by-claim PRISMA/AMSTAR-2/GRADE. ALREADY CODIFIED. (5) ITERATE claude<->codex = §8.3 + REVIEW_BRIEF_FORMAT §7 iter-N awareness + §8.3.5 iteration_trajectory.md + §8.3.9 output schema. Workflow primitive: structured-verdict schema + iteration_trajectory append + the `loop`/`Monitor` tools to wait on Codex. ALREADY CODIFIED. (6) IF APPROVE OR 5-ITER CAP = §8.3.1 hard cap of 5 + force-APPROVE artifact procedure (codex_brief_verdict.txt / codex_diff_audit.txt final `verdict: APPROVE` line). ALREADY CODIFIED VERBATIM. (7) CLOSE THE GH ISSUE = §3.0/§10/§8.2 — this is the ONE place the literal operator phrasing must be reframed (see pitfalls): Claude does NOT merge/close; Codex APPROVE -> CI `polaris/codex-required` passes -> auto-merge -> issue auto-closes; §-1.2 step 7 'Close the GitHub Issue when the PR merges' is the mechanical follow-through, not a Claude admin action. (8) MOVE TO NEXT GH ISSUE IN STRICT SEQUENCE (military order) = §8.3.10 'immediate next action is git checkout -b bot/<next-issue>' + §3.0 'cannot start Issue N+1 until Issue N completed' + issue_breakdown.md locked sequence. ALREADY CODIFIED; operator 'military order' DISAMBIGUATES the §10-step-4 'do not pick autonomously' tension (see findings below).

CONFLICTS / DUPLICATES (deliverable 2). The operator's prompt itself names the three DUPLICATES, all confirmed against source: (a) the 5-cap already lives in CLAUDE.md §8.3.1 (verbatim canonical block) + REVIEW_BRIEF_FORMAT §0 references it; (b) the issue-driven strict sequence already lives in §3.0 + §8.3.10 + issue_breakdown.md; (c) smoke-before-PR/sweep already lives in §-1.2 step 3 + scripts/dr_benchmark/smoke.md. These are NOT new — the Workflow practice must REFERENCE them, not restate or fork them. GENUINELY NEW (3 items): (i) the Workflow-tool ORCHESTRATION LAYER that automates the Claude-side loop sequencing (the substrate that produced .codex/I-meta-002/feasibility_synthesis.md via fan-out + adversarial-verify + convergence_call) — this is the per-Issue Codex-review loop, NOT the separate K=5 brief-blinded rotating-lens AUDIT_CYCLE_PROTOCOL; (ii) the smoke-BEFORE-SPEND gate (deliverable 4) as an explicit money/GPU guard placed between build and convey; (iii) visual-to-Codex generalized from the UI-only §9 / 6th-artifact codex_visual_audit.txt path into a standing 'convey all results incl visual' step.

ONE REAL INTERNAL TENSION TO RESOLVE (not raw conflict): §8.3.10 mandates auto-advance ('immediate next action = checkout -b bot/<next-issue>') while §10 step 4 + §8.2 say 'do NOT pick a task autonomously; user assigns via TaskCreate' and ban active_issue.json transition before assignment. RESOLUTION the operator's 'strict military order' directive supplies: because state/polaris_restart/issue_breakdown.md is a LOCKED sequence, 'next' is never a discretionary pick, so mechanical advance is honored. The §10/§8.2 'no autonomous pick' rule survives narrowed to DISCRETIONARY choice when ordering is ambiguous — it does not block following the locked sequence. This is operator-resolves-pre-existing-ambiguity, not operator-overrides-cage.

12-STEP ANTI-DRIFT ORDER AS A REUSABLE PREAMBLE/CHECKLIST (deliverable 3). The 12 steps are the I-meta-001 (#933) implementation order, and most are CONDITIONAL — they fire only when a PR touches the relevant surface (the architecture-conformance.yml `paths:` filter is the trigger; non-arch PRs skip cleanly). Reusable applies-when checklist, tied to existing automation: 1 branch-hygiene (branch == active_issue_id; checked by weekly_drift_report.check_branch_active_issue_conformance); 2 decision-doc tracking (no untracked _lock/_sota/_pick/_audit docs; weekly_drift_report.find_untracked_decision_docs); 3 status:superseded frontmatter on stale model refs (weekly_drift_report.find_stale_model_refs); 4 runtime lock = config/architecture/polaris_runtime_lock.yaml is source of truth; 5 propagation manifest (verify_lock.check_propagation_manifest — all checkpoints true to promote pending->locked); 6 canonical pin tracks the lock SHA (canonical_pin_includes_this_file checkpoint); 7 family registry expansion + N-way all_distinct check (verify_lock.verify_lock_against_code against _FAMILY_PREFIXES); 8 distinct per-role env vars (PG_GENERATOR/MIRROR/SENTINEL/JUDGE_MODEL; PG_EVALUATOR_MODEL legacy-compat maps_to_role mirror, deprecated 2026-09-06); 9 pathB gate architecture-coverage (pathB_run_gate._assert_architecture_coverage refuses smokes while status=codex_approved_pending_operator_signature); 10 update stale surfaces (.env.example, agent_architecture.md, architecture.md — weekly_drift_report); 11 PR attestation artifact (.codex/<id>/architecture_conformance_attestation.md from the template; CI gate architecture-conformance-required parses the 7 required-true assertions + lock_sha256 + Codex trail paths); 12 weekly drift report (scripts/architecture/weekly_drift_report.py, schedulable via CronCreate). Steps 4-7 are automated by verify_lock.py; steps 1,2,3,10,12 by weekly_drift_report.py; step 11 by the CI gate. Folds in as: run the conditional subset whose trigger surface the current Issue touches, as a preamble checklist before opening the PR.

SMOKE-BEFORE-SPEND GATE (deliverable 4) — WHERE + WHAT PASSES WITHOUT GPU SPEND. Location in the loop: between 'claude build' and 'convey to codex', gating the expensive path. Expensive path it guards: full scripts/run_honest_sweep_r3 (PG_SWEEP_FETCH_CAP=500 fetches, PG_LIVE_MAX_EV_TO_GEN=300, 5x$0-$40/run per smoke.md) PLUS sustained Vast.ai GPU rental for self-hosted Mirror/Sentinel/Judge (I-meta-002 feasibility: Judge 1xH100 FP8, Sentinel 1xA100/4090, Mirror 2xH100 w4a4 or Cohere-direct). PASSING connectivity+functionality smoke that requires NO expensive spend: (a) unit/fixture tests — pathB_run_gate.py docstring 'Pure-logic core is fixture-tested with NO live system' => pytest tests/dr_benchmark/ tests/architecture/; (b) python -m scripts.architecture.verify_lock report exit 0 (lock/family/propagation consistency, zero network); (c) pathB_run_gate.preflight(offline=True) — skips network reachability AND architecture-coverage enforcement, validates wiring shape only; (d) minimal REAL connectivity calls = real_retrieval_prober (one Serper 'metformin' POST + one Semantic Scholar 1-result GET) + one tiny per-role LLM call to confirm the output CONTRACT not the full run: Sentinel polarity test (yes=UNGROUNDED, the lethal §-1.1 inversion), Judge guided_choice 5-enum, Mirror two-pass citation/JSON. CRITICAL CURRENT INTERACTION: while polaris_runtime_lock.yaml status == codex_approved_pending_operator_signature, pathB_run_gate.py:311-317 _assert_architecture_coverage RAISES GateError and REFUSES ALL non-offline smokes — so the live 4-role smoke-before-spend path is FROZEN BY DESIGN right now; only the offline/unit smoke (a-c) runs until verify_lock promotes the lock to status: locked. This freeze is a citable consequence, not a gap.
recommendations: CODIFY THE LOOP AS 'WORKFLOW ORCHESTRATES CLAUDE-SIDE WORK; GATES KEEP AUTHORITY.' Write the standing-loop doc so each stage explicitly DELEGATES to the existing rule by section number (build->§3.0; smoke->§-1.2 step3 + smoke.md; convey->§8.3.3 + REVIEW_BRIEF_FORMAT; review->§3.0 + red_team_checklist + §-1.1; iterate->§8.3 + iteration_trajectory.md; cap->§8.3.1; advance->§8.3.10 + issue_breakdown.md). The Workflow tool drives SEQUENCING and the Claude-side build/smoke/convey/iterate cycle; it never owns a gate decision. This is the single sentence that makes the practice EXTEND not contradict.

REWRITE THE 'CLOSE THE ISSUE' STAGE so it never reads as a Claude action. Correct chain: Codex APPROVE (or §8.3.1 force-APPROVE at iter 5) -> codex_brief_verdict.txt/codex_diff_audit.txt final `verdict: APPROVE` line -> CI required check polaris/codex-required passes -> GitHub auto-merge -> issue auto-closes -> Workflow checks out bot/<next-issue>. Claude has NO gh pr merge --admin (CHARTER §1, forbidden_admin_merge, forbidden_autonomous_merge_is_cage_bypass). NOTE the live caveat from active_issue.json D4: polaris main currently has required_status_checks=[] and enforce_admins=false, so codex-required is NOT yet enforced as a required check — flag this as an operator-side branch-protection fix the auto-close chain depends on.

STATE THE 'MILITARY ORDER' RESOLUTION EXPLICITLY in the doc: strict-sequence advance is mechanical because issue_breakdown.md is locked; §10-step-4 'don't pick autonomously' is narrowed to discretionary picks when order is ambiguous, and does NOT license skipping the locked next-issue. This pre-empts the §8.2/§8.3.10 vs §10 tension being read as a contradiction.

PRESENT THE 12-STEP ORDER AS A CONDITIONAL APPLIES-WHEN PREAMBLE keyed to the architecture-conformance.yml paths: filter, not a mandatory 12-action ritual per task. Bind each step to its automation (verify_lock.py for 4-7; weekly_drift_report.py for 1,2,3,10,12; attestation template + architecture-conformance-required CI gate for 11) so the checklist is mostly 'run these scripts + confirm green', making it reusable across any future lock-touching Issue.

INSERT THE SMOKE-BEFORE-SPEND GATE as an explicit pre-conveyance step with two tiers: TIER-1 offline (always runs, no spend): pytest tests/dr_benchmark tests/architecture + verify_lock report + preflight(offline=True); TIER-2 minimal-real connectivity (cheap, no full sweep, no sustained GPU): retrieval prober pings + one per-role contract call. Only after both pass does the expensive full sweep / Vast.ai rental fire. Document that Tier-2 live 4-role smoke is currently BLOCKED by the operator freeze (lock pending) and unblocks when verify_lock promotes status to locked.

GENERALIZE VISUAL-TO-CODEX from the UI-only path (REVIEW_BRIEF_FORMAT §9 + 6th artifact codex_visual_audit.txt + scripts/visual_review_gate.py + codex exec -i) into a standing 'convey ALL results incl screenshots/render evidence' loop step, but reuse the existing artifact + rubric machinery (visual_audit_rubric.md, codex-visual-required CI gate) rather than inventing a parallel path; non-visual Issues skip cleanly as the gate already does.
pitfalls: CAGE-CLOSE TRAP (highest severity): the literal operator phrasing 'close the GH issue -> move to next' reads as Claude closing/advancing autonomously. If the mapping writes 'Claude closes the issue', it INVERTS the deliverable's purpose and reproduces the failure_28_commits / autonomous-merge-is-cage-bypass anti-pattern. Claude never merges/closes; the gate chain does. This is the one item that, if missed, breaks 'EXTENDS not contradicts'.

DO NOT CONFLATE the per-Issue Codex review loop (REVIEW_BRIEF_FORMAT + §8.3, the operator's loop) with the SEPARATE K=5 brief-blinded rotating-lens AUDIT_CYCLE_PROTOCOL (continuous post-commit cycle audit). They are different mechanisms; the operator's loop is the per-Issue gate.

SENTINEL POLARITY is §-1.1-LETHAL: Granite Guardian 4.1 emits yes=UNGROUNDED/hallucinated, no=grounded. Any smoke-before-spend per-role contract test MUST assert this mapping; inverting it lets fabrications pass a clinical gate. (I-meta-002 feasibility_synthesis §1.2 + §6 risk 3.)

THE LIVE 4-ROLE SMOKE IS FROZEN BY DESIGN right now (pathB_run_gate.py:311-317 raises while lock status=codex_approved_pending_operator_signature). Do not present the live smoke-before-spend path as currently runnable; only offline/unit smoke runs until the lock is promoted to locked via verify_lock.py propagation checkpoints.

JUDGE SLUG TYPO persists in the lock (line 77 qwen/qwen-3.6-35b-a3b vs correct qwen/qwen3.6-35b-a3b); the smoke per-role contract call for the Judge would fail / the coverage gate cannot observe the role until the operator-signed lock-mutation Issue fixes it. Don't assume the lock is internally consistent.

DON'T MANUFACTURE EXTRA CONFLICTS: the prompt already enumerates the duplicate set (5-cap, sequence, smoke) and the new set (Workflow layer, smoke-before-spend, visual). Honor that enumeration; the only ADDITIONAL tension worth surfacing is the cage-close one and the §8.3.10-vs-§10 advance ambiguity.
